title: Consumption Telemetry Provenance and Crediting Specification
preview:
Summary:
This design spec defines a single-source-of-truth for consumption telemetry provenance and the rules for crediting usage against customer contracts. The goal is to ensure that downstream billing, SLO-to-cost mapping, fidelity gating, and customer-facing dashboards all rely on consistent, auditable telemetry...
text:
Summary:
This design spec defines a single-source-of-truth for consumption telemetry provenance and the rules for crediting usage against customer contracts. The goal is to ensure that downstream billing, SLO-to-cost mapping, fidelity gating, and customer-facing dashboards all rely on consistent, auditable telemetry with deterministic crediting rules.

Audience:
- Product managers (pricing, packaging)
- Billing & finance engineers
- Observability and telemetry engineers
- Serving/runtime engineers
- Solutions and customer success teams owning onboarding

Goals:
1) Unambiguous provenance: every consumption event must include fields that allow any system to determine which contract, route, and fidelity setting should be charged.
2) Deterministic crediting: define the precedence rules and transformations used to map raw events to billable units and credits.
3) Minimal invasive instrument...

title: optimize-mini-conductor-launch-notes-2029-11-21

Mini-Conductor is a companion to the existing Optimize ...
text:
Overview
--------
This release ships Optimize Mini-Conductor v1.0 — a lightweight, opinionated runtime and advisor for cost-aware fidelity management targeted at latency-sensitive and bandwidth-constrained usage (mobile, edge proxies, and small VPC footprints). Mini-Conductor is a companion to the existing Optimize Conductor product family: it provides a reduced dependency surface, offline-capable policies, and deterministic fallback behaviors for customers who need predictable economics at the 1–10 QPS range.

Goals of this release
- Provide a compact policy engine that can run in a single small VM/container alongside a local inference proxy.
- Offer deterministic fidelity fallbacks and local caching to guarantee latency SLOs under intermittent connectivity.
- Expose a developer-friendly SDK and Console hooks for telemetry and policy tuning.
- Ensure a safe rollout path with canary f...

title: Consumption SLA Mapping Playbook: route-to-fidelity and migration guidance

text:
Purpose and scope:

This playbook defines how to map per-route customer SLAs (latency targets, cost ceilings, and fidelity budgets) to Redwood consumption primitives (serving tiers, model variants, caching policies, and quantization profiles). It provides a prescriptive migration and rollout plan for customers moving from coarse global tiers to route- or feature-level fidelity controls.

Intended audience:
- Product managers owning consumption tiers and billable features
- Solutions and customer engineering teams executing migrations
- Backend engineers and runtime owners implementing route-level enforcement
- Finance and pricing for estimating revenue/usage impacts

High-level goals:
- Provide a repeatable mapping from customer SLA statements to Redwood feature configuration (tier, model variant, cache policy).
- Minimize customer-perceived regression risk when shifting fidelity at r...

title: Adaptive Session Compression: Product Brief and Roadmap
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This brief describes the product concept, technical approach, rollout plan, and commercial considerations for "Adaptive Session Compression" — a Redwood Optimize adjunct designed to reduce per-session token cost and memory pressure while preserving conversational fidelity for production deployment...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This brief describes the product concept, technical approach, rollout plan, and commercial considerations for "Adaptive Session Compression" — a Redwood Optimize adjunct designed to reduce per-session token cost and memory pressure while preserving conversational fidelity for production deployments.

Scope: This document covers customer-facing behavior, high-level runtime design, telemetry and success metrics, staged rollout plan, and pricing/packaging options. Implementation-level kernel selection and per-hardware optimizations are covered in a separate runtime design spec (see related pages).

Problem statement

Redwood customers running long conversational sessions (median history > 2k tokens) experience two correlated pain points:

- Rising per-request token cost as sessions grow without bound.
- Latency and memory pressure on inference nodes caused by large KV ...

title: SLO-driven fidelity onboarding checklist and playbook

text:
Overview

This document is a pragmatic playbook for onboarding customers onto SLO-driven fidelity tiers. It describes the mapping between customer SLOs (latency, availability, correctness), recommended fidelity tiers, required telemetry, operational checks, and a step-by-step checklist for commercial and technical leads. Use this when contracting customers for dedicated or private deployments and when migrating high-usage tenants from hosted to dedicated environments.

Audience

- Product managers and solutions engineering
- Customer success and account teams
- Platform and runtime engineers (serving, routing, telemetry)

Goals

1. Provide a repeatable alignment process that converts customer SLAs into recommended fidelity packages.
2. Ensure operational readiness (telemetry, runbooks, rollback) before committing to a fidelity onboarding date.
3. Minimize regressions in perceived qual...

title: Pragmatic PRD: Fidelity Cost Adapter for Variant Marketplace
preview:
Summary:

This PRD describes the Fidelity Cost Adapter (FCA): a lightweight service + UX layer that maps customer-defined fidelity selections in the Variant Marketplace to precise serving configurations and cost signals. The FCA enables customers and the billing pipeline to reconcile chosen fidelity levels (e.g., to...
text:
Summary:

This PRD describes the Fidelity Cost Adapter (FCA): a lightweight service + UX layer that maps customer-defined fidelity selections in the Variant Marketplace to precise serving configurations and cost signals. The FCA enables customers and the billing pipeline to reconcile chosen fidelity levels (e.g., token fidelity, quantization, batching profile) with runtime routing choices, unit economics, and invoice line items. This PRD covers requirements, API examples, acceptance criteria, rollout strategy, and pricing implications.

Goals and success metrics:

- Goal 1: Provide a deterministic mapping from fidelity selection -> serving configuration and billing code.
 - Success metric: 99.9% deterministic mapping coverage for active marketplace variants within 4 weeks of GA.
- Goal 2: Reduce variance between estimated and actual per-request cost for fidelity-enabled routes.
 - Suc...

title: Model Consumption Policy and Customer Mapping Playbook
preview:

It is intended for product managers, solutions engineers, pricing, and account teams executing migrations o...
text:
Overview:

This playbook defines Redwood's Model Consumption Policy and a repeatable customer mapping process that converts product-level fidelity and consumption choices into billing, SLOs, and rollout plans. It is intended for product managers, solutions engineers, pricing, and account teams executing migrations or packaging changes that affect per-route fidelity, token accounting, and unit economics.

Goals:
- Create a single, auditable mapping from customer consumption intent -> fidelity tier -> pricing and SLO bundle.
- Reduce customer surprises during migrations or packaging changes by providing prescriptive migration steps and verification checks.
- Enable automated route-level enforcement and telemetry for consumption-driven decisions.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and dedicated customers; private deployments must follow the same contract template but may defer enforcement to cu...

title: Fidelity Decision Service (FDS) + Client SDK PRD and Requirements

text:
Overview:

This document defines the product requirements and high-level design for the Fidelity Decision Service (FDS) and its companion Client SDK. FDS is a low-latency decisioning layer that selects per-request fidelity configurations (model variant, quantization profile, batching class, and routing policy) to meet tenant SLOs and cost constraints while preserving output quality. The Client SDK exposes a compact API for applications to express intent and enforcement boundaries.

Audience:
- Product managers and feature owners responsible for fidelity and cost controls
- Runtime and serving engineering teams implementing decisioning logic
- Developer experience and SDK teams building client integrations
- SRE and capacity planning teams evaluating cost and throughput impacts

Problem statement and motivation:
- Customers want predictable latency and cost while maintaining quality. T...

title: Console Usage Insights 2.0 - Release Notes and Migration Guide
preview:

The goal of 2.0 is to improve per-route observability, reduce noisy telemetry ingestion cost, and p...
text:
Overview

This document describes the Console Usage Insights 2.0 release (codename: Insightful), the user-facing features, operational changes, breaking/deprecation items, and a migration plan for teams and customers. The goal of 2.0 is to improve per-route observability, reduce noisy telemetry ingestion cost, and provide a first-class drilldown from token-level cost to caller traces.

Highlights (what's new)

- Token-level cost breakdown: per-request token attribution across model, batching, and caching stages in the Console UI and API.
- Route timeline explorer: interactive per-route latency and cost histograms with configurable zoom ranges and percentile overlays.
- Trace integration: native links to Redwood traces and automatic span correlation for Console-visible requests.
- Retention & sampling changes: default storage retention for usage events changes from 90 days to 30 days f...

title: Unified Capacity Transition PRD: SLO-aligned Consumption and Dedicated Migration
preview:
Executive summary:

This document defines the product requirements, design constraints, rollout roadmap, pricing impacts, and release notes for the "Unified Capacity Transition" initiative. The initiative enables customers to move from legacy per-token consumption and ad-hoc reserved capacity models to an SLO-aligne...
text:
Executive summary:

This document defines the product requirements, design constraints, rollout roadmap, pricing impacts, and release notes for the "Unified Capacity Transition" initiative. The initiative enables customers to move from legacy per-token consumption and ad-hoc reserved capacity models to an SLO-aligned consumption paradigm that unifies Dedicated reservations, hosted usage, and private deployments under a single migration playbook.

Goals:
- Provide a predictable migration path for customers on Dedicated capacity and legacy token billing to SLO-aligned bundles.
- Maintain or improve customer latency SLOs (p50 <= 45ms for short sequences, p95 <= 250ms for chat workloads) during and after migration.
- Prevent revenue regressions and make pricing transparent to customers.
- Reduce operational overhead for account teams via automated migration tooling, billing reconciliation...

title: Predictive Bundling & SLA Credit Algorithm Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the predictive bundling model and SLA crediting algorithm used to (1) recommend bundled credit products for tenants, (2) apply automated SLA credits when latency or availability SLOs are missed, and (3) simulate the downstream financial impact of different bundling strategies. The go...
text:
Overview:

This playbook defines the predictive bundling model and SLA crediting algorithm used to (1) recommend bundled credit products for tenants, (2) apply automated SLA credits when latency or availability SLOs are missed, and (3) simulate the downstream financial impact of different bundling strategies. The goal is to enable predictable customer billing while preserving fairness and operational incentives across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployment modes.

Key objectives:
- Provide a transparent, auditable algorithm for SLA credit calculations tied to measurable telemetry signals.
- Surface predictive bundle recommendations that balance expected token consumption, latency SLOs, and cost-to-serve.
- Offer a simulation harness (offline and lightweight online mode) to estimate revenue and credit exposure before packaging changes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Hosted API customers a...

title: PRD: Developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API
preview:
Overview

Purpose
This PRD defines a developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API that enables Redwood customers to request, negotiate, and commit to output fidelity levels at request time. The API exposes deterministic negotiation primitives for: 1) proposing a preferred fidelity (quality/cost tradeoff), 2) receiving ...
text:
Overview

Purpose
This PRD defines a developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API that enables Redwood customers to request, negotiate, and commit to output fidelity levels at request time. The API exposes deterministic negotiation primitives for: 1) proposing a preferred fidelity (quality/cost tradeoff), 2) receiving a server-side fidelity offer (with expected latency and cost estimate), and 3) committing to a selected fidelity before generation begins.

Why this matters
- Reduces overprovisioned latency and cost by making fidelity an explicit contract between client and server.
- Enables finer-grained observability and billing alignment: requests are tagged with negotiated fidelity and can be aggregated for SLOs and chargeback.
- Supports graceful degradation strategies (fallbacks, cached responses) with clear semantic guarantees to clients.

Goals (MUST)
- Provide a simple REST/SDK su...

title: Enterprise fidelity surcharge and grace period policy
preview:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or rese...
text:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or reserved warm capacity) while giving a clear transition window for customers migrating across fidelity bands.

Summary (one paragraph)
Enterprise customers may opt into fidelity guarantees for mission-critical routes. When a customer selects a fidelity guarantee that increases runtime cost materially (as defined in the Surcharge Thresholds table), a fidelity surcharge may be applied to their invoice. Customers receive a configurable grace period during onboarding or migration where surcharges are delayed, partly discounted, or phased in to reduce churn and provide time to optim...

title: per-route-slo-creditization-and-billing-prd-2027-09-15
preview:
Overview

This PRD defines the Per-Route SLO Creditization feature: a metering and billing model that maps customer-defined SLOs and route-level policies to fractional credit allocations. The goal is to let engineering teams and finance owners tie SLAs (latency, availability, error budget) directly to a customer’s c...
text:
Overview

This PRD defines the Per-Route SLO Creditization feature: a metering and billing model that maps customer-defined SLOs and route-level policies to fractional credit allocations. The goal is to let engineering teams and finance owners tie SLAs (latency, availability, error budget) directly to a customer’s committed consumption bundle so that billing reflects the cost of meeting higher SLOs without forcing coarse-grained reserved capacity purchases.

Motivation and background

Problem: Customers currently choose between broad reserved capacity (Dedicated) or pay-as-you-go hosted pricing. There is no first-class way to express that route / model X requires 99.9% latency SLO at 50 RPS while route Y is best-effort and can be served by cheaper fallbacks. This leads to overprovisioning, unpredictable bills, and inefficient routing decisions.

Opportunity: Introduce a per-route cred...

title: Embeddings Retention & Tiered Storage PRD
preview:
Overview:

This PRD defines a system for configurable retention and tiered storage for customer embeddings stored in Redwood Inference. The goal is to reduce per-customer storage cost, control long-term operational expense, and provide clear controls for compliance (data deletion/retention) while preserving retrieva...
text:
Overview:

This PRD defines a system for configurable retention and tiered storage for customer embeddings stored in Redwood Inference. The goal is to reduce per-customer storage cost, control long-term operational expense, and provide clear controls for compliance (data deletion/retention) while preserving retrieval latency and quality for production retrieval workloads.

Background and problem statement:

- Customers increasingly store large collections of vectors (embeddings) for semantic search, personalization, agent memory, and analytics.
- Hot storage costs are rising as vector stores grow; many vectors are infrequently accessed but must often be retained for regulatory or business reasons.
- Current single-tier storage forces a cost/latency tradeoff: keep everything hot (low latency, high cost) or move to cold (low cost, high latency and reindexing overhead).
- Operators need ...

title: PRD: Developer Simulation Kit & Telemetry Bridge for Preflight Testing
preview:
Summary

This PRD proposes a Developer Simulation Kit (DevSim) and a Telemetry Bridge that together provide engineers with an offline, high-fidelity preflight environment for: workload simulation, latency/cost forecasting, fidelity experiments, and telemetry capture that mirrors production signals. The goal is to re...
text:
Summary

This PRD proposes a Developer Simulation Kit (DevSim) and a Telemetry Bridge that together provide engineers with an offline, high-fidelity preflight environment for: workload simulation, latency/cost forecasting, fidelity experiments, and telemetry capture that mirrors production signals. The goal is to reduce time-to-safe-deploy for engine and product teams by catching regressions and cost surprises earlier.

Context and motivation

- Teams repeatedly run into late-stage surprises when moving workloads from test harnesses to hosted/dedicated/private environments: unexpected tail-latency spikes, different token costs due to batching/caching behavior, and gaps in observability.
- Current sandboxes either (a) are lightweight and fast but underestimate cost/latency, or (b) are production clones that are expensive and slow to provision.
- DevSim + Telemetry Bridge is a developer...

title: SLO-driven prefetch adapter PRD: prefetch-adapter-design-and-acceptance-criteria

Title: SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter (PRD) — prefetch-adapter-design-and-acceptance-criteria

Summary:
This PRD defines the requirements, success criteria, and rollout plan for the SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter: a lightweight runtime component that proactively primes model KV caches, session state, and short-lived model...
text:
Title: SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter (PRD) — prefetch-adapter-design-and-acceptance-criteria

Summary:
This PRD defines the requirements, success criteria, and rollout plan for the SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter: a lightweight runtime component that proactively primes model KV caches, session state, and short-lived model variants based on per-route SLOs, observed traffic shape, and cost constraints. The Adapter's goal is to reduce tail latency for critical routes while keeping incremental cost within configured budgets.

Overview and motivation
- Problem: Some customer routes (interactive chat, autocomplete) experience intermittent tail latency and cold-KV misses that push requests over their SLOs. Reactive strategies (retry/backoff, fallbacks) mitigate user impact but do not eliminate user-visible latency spikes.
- Opportunity: We can trade a small, predictable additional inference cost f...

title: Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop - Design Spec
preview:
Overview

This design specification describes the Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop (AIFL), a runtime-to-control-plane closed loop that continuously observes production signals, evaluates fidelity and cost/latency tradeoffs, and proposes or applies configuration updates to the serving runtime. The goal is to improve ...
text:
Overview

This design specification describes the Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop (AIFL), a runtime-to-control-plane closed loop that continuously observes production signals, evaluates fidelity and cost/latency tradeoffs, and proposes or applies configuration updates to the serving runtime. The goal is to improve unit economics while preserving or improving customer-facing quality and latency SLOs.

Goals
- Reduce per-token cost by 10–30% on workloads with stable patterns through automated batching, local-prefetch adjustments, and selective quantization fallbacks.
- Prevent customer-facing quality regressions: automatic changes must not increase the evaluation regression score (see Quality Profiler) beyond configurable thresholds (default: 0.5% absolute for high-importance prompt sets).
- Honor latency SLOs: p95 latency must remain below tenant-configured SLO (default 300 ms for int...

text:
Summary
-------
This playbook defines policies, automated controls, and an operational checklist for handling surge events that impact Redwood service tiers (Developer, Standard, Reserved). It focuses on minimizing customer-visible failures, preserving SLOs, and making predictable commercial decisions (credits, throttles, and fallback). This document combines product requirements, rollout checklist, runbook actions, and pricing impact guidelines for engineering, product, SRE, and GTM teams.

Goals
-----
- Maintain 99th percentile latency SLOs for each tier when possible.
- Provide deterministic fallback behavior (variant, fidelity, region) within 200ms decision latency.
- Ensure cost/backstop actions are transparent and reversible.
- Reduce manual escalation during surges through automation and clear ownership.

Scope and definitions
---------------------
- Surge event: a tenant- or s...

title: Fidelity Scaling Advisor — product brief and rollout playbook
preview:

FSA is an in-product advisor that helps customers choose and automatically apply fidelity (model variant, quantiza...
text:
Overview

This document describes the Fidelity Scaling Advisor (FSA): a product brief, high-level requirements, design constraints, pricing & packaging guidance, rollout plan, and initial release notes. FSA is an in-product advisor that helps customers choose and automatically apply fidelity (model variant, quantization, batching, and prefix-caching) and consumption settings to meet target cost, latency, or quality objectives. FSA is intended to complement Redwood Optimize and the Console recommendations by providing an opinionated, lightweight decision service surfaced at the point of consumption.

Goals

- Help customers systematically trade cost, latency, and output fidelity with minimal manual tuning.
- Reduce per-token spend for common production workloads by 15–40% without measurable quality regressions for supported workloads.
- Provide transparent decision rationale (why a can...

title: Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant — Product Brief
preview:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-...
text:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-driven heuristics, and in-console prescriptive actions so teams can move from exploratory API usage to production-safe consumption with minimal manual tuning.

Goals

- Reduce time-to-safe-production for developer integrations by detecting fidelity vs. cost regressions during onboarding and early ramp.
- Provide actionable remediation suggestions (config changes, candidate model variants, quantization presets) scoped to a customer’s observed workload.
- Integrate with Console, SDKs, and CI pipelines: capture preflight checks, post-deploy regressions, and staged rollout gate...

It combines step-by-step onboarding flows, telemetry and SLO mappings, configuration snippets, and rollout guidance so teams can: 1) accelerate developer time-to-first-call, 2) surface accurate consumption estimates early, and 3) collect telemetry that enables billing and product analytics.

 - Reduce common developer confusion around fidelity, model variants, and warm capacity.
- Non-goals:

title: Interactive Cost Signal Orchestrator (Design Spec)
preview:
## Overview

This design spec describes the Interactive Cost Signal Orchestrator (ICSO): a lightweight, runtime-oriented control plane that ingests low-latency cost and fidelity signals and emits per-request routing and fidelity directives for interactive LLM applications. ICSO is intended to be used by Hosted, Dedi...
text:
## Overview

This design spec describes the Interactive Cost Signal Orchestrator (ICSO): a lightweight, runtime-oriented control plane that ingests low-latency cost and fidelity signals and emits per-request routing and fidelity directives for interactive LLM applications. ICSO is intended to be used by Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments to reduce unit cost while preserving customer SLOs in conversational and transactional workloads.

ICSO coordinates with the serving runtime, the policy store, and the telemetry pipeline to make deterministic, auditable decisions that can be tuned per-tenant and per-route.

## Goals

- Reduce average cost-per-user-interaction by up to 18% for targeted conversational routes without increasing 95th-percentile latency by more than 5%.
- Provide a declarative policy model that product teams can use to bind cost/fidelity rules to routes and tenants...

title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD
preview:
Title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD

Overview:
This PRD describes the user-facing and technical requirements for a Graceful Fidelity Degradation feature: a platform-level capability that reduces model output fidelity in controlled ways when customer-configured SLOs (latency, error rate, cost budget) are at risk...
text:
Title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD

Overview:
This PRD describes the user-facing and technical requirements for a Graceful Fidelity Degradation feature: a platform-level capability that reduces model output fidelity in controlled ways when customer-configured SLOs (latency, error rate, cost budget) are at risk. The objective is to preserve availability and acceptable latency by switching to lower-fidelity model variants, structured output with summarization, or token-budgeted responses while guaranteeing predictable cost and automated rollback when pressure abates.

Background and problem statement:
- Problem: Production applications using Redwood Inference occasionally face capacity or latency pressure (regional outages, batch-surge traffic, cold-starts for private deployments). Current fallbacks are coarse (full model variant swap or throttling) and can cause large quality reg...

title: variant-marketplace-prd-catalog-and-addon-pricing-2026-09-10
preview:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by varian...
text:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by variant and supports metered add-ons to improve unit economics without sacrificing discoverability or operational controls.

Context and motivation:
- Customers increasingly want to choose model tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) without building custom model ops.
- Current catalog is binary (model version). We need a marketplace UX + billing contract that encodes variant attributes and add-on metering.
- This unlocks: explicit pricing for lower-cost quantized variants, premium pricing for ultra-low-latency variants on dedicated capacity, and per-customer curated variant bund...

Overview

Summary:
The Shadow Evaluator is a product initiative to provide an automated, prompt-set driven regression detection service that runs shadow (non-production-impacting) evaluations against candidate model variants prior to rollouts. The goal is to catch regressions in real-world prompt patterns and alert product and model owners with actionable diagnostics before customer-facing traffic is exposed.

This PRD defines user journeys, functional and non-functional requirements, high-level design, rollout plan, success metrics, and operational run/playbook for the feature.

Motivation and context:
- Customers and internal teams increasingly rely on model variant rollouts and automated canaries. Existing unit-test and lab evals miss distributional edge-cases embedded in customer prompt-sets.
- We need an evaluation layer that: 1) reuses customer prompt-sets (curated + captured), ...

text:
Summary
-------
This PRD defines the product scope, customer contracts, and operational controls required to introduce first‑party Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for latency across Redwood’s Hosted and Dedicated surfaces. The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency targets, (b) bind those targets to billing/credit commitments, and (c) opt into automated route shaping and fallback policies that protect higher‑value traffic.

Goals and non‑goals
------------------
Primary goals:
- Provide a clear, enforceable lifecycle for per‑route latency SLOs (declare → observe → enforce → invoice).
- Enable commercial contracts that map latency tiers to pricing credits and capacity reservations.
- Offer runtime controls (shaping, prefetched capacity, graceful degradation) so customers achieve SLOs without manual operations.

Non‑goals for this PRD:
...

title: Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator — Product Brief and Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This document describes the Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator (SLA Composer). The SLA Composer is a productized authoring UX and backend simulator that lets sales, solutions engineering, and customers design, preview, and validate SLA-driven consumption contracts for conversational worklo...
text:
Overview:

This document describes the Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator (SLA Composer). The SLA Composer is a productized authoring UX and backend simulator that lets sales, solutions engineering, and customers design, preview, and validate SLA-driven consumption contracts for conversational workloads. It couples a declarative contract DSL, a simulation runner (SimRunner), and a telemetry-aware estimator to produce latency, cost, and quality projections for candidate contracts before signing.

Goals:
- Provide a single surface for creating vendor- and tenant-facing SLA contracts for conversational applications (chat, interactive assistants, streaming responses).
- Enable realistic pre-sale simulations matching customer's traffic shape to predict p50/p95 latency distribution, token costs, and fallback frequency.
- Reduce contract churn by surfacing trade-offs (batchin...

title: PRD: Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime — API, SLOs, Pricing, and Rollout
preview:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution,...
text:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-...

## Summary
We’re rolling out a **beta** of **In-console Usage Insights** in the Redwood Console. This release adds route- and model-level breakdowns to help engineering and finance stakeholders identify the biggest cost drivers (tokens, cache behavior, retries/fallbacks, and context length patterns) and reconcile usage with billing.

**Who this is for:** engineering leads, platform teams, and FinOps partners who need reliable cost attribution by API route and model.

**Where to find it:** Console → **Usage & Cost** → **Usage Insights (Beta)**

---

## What’s included in this beta
### 1) Route + model cost & usage breakdown
You can now view a breakdown such as:
- **Route**: `/v1/chat/completions` (and other Redwood + OpenAI-compat endpoints)
- **Model**: `llama-3.1-70b` (with stable family/version bucketing)

Breakdowns include:
- **Total cost** and **usage** by time bucket
- **Tokens ...

## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **internal policy** for:

- **Included usage** and what counts toward it
- **Overage behavior** (what happens at/above limits)
- How billing **meters map** to packages

This policy exists to ensure consistent packaging, predictable customer experience, and audit-ready billing behavior.

**Effective date:** 2026-03-01 (for new signups). Existing customers transition at renewal or via signed order form amendment.

---

## Scope
Applies to Redwood **Hosted API** usage purchased via self-serve or order form:
- Text generation / chat
- Embeddings
- Reranking (where enabled)

Does **not** apply to:
- Redwood Dedicated (see Dedicated metering pages)
- Redwood Private licensing

This beta is targeted at:

The goal is to prevent silent quality regressions fro...
text:
## Summary
Quality Gates (Beta) adds mandatory evaluation-based guardrails before Redwood Optimize recommendations can be applied to production traffic.

This beta is targeted at:
- Internal routes/workloads
- A limited set of hosted API customers (by allowlist)

The goal is to prevent silent quality regressions from cost optimizations (batching, caching/KV behavior, and quantization profile changes) while keeping the Optimize workflow fast and auditable.

---

## Availability
**Deployment scope (Beta):** Hosted API (selected routes/accounts). Dedicated is “manual + documented” during beta.

**Default behavior:**
- Optimize recommendations that are eligible for one-click apply will now require a passing gate run.
- Auto-apply policies (where enabled) will not execute unless gates pass.

**Console surface area:** Gate status and results are visible in the Optimize recommendation detail...

title: PRD: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration (Rollouts + SLO Monitors)
preview:
## Overview
Redwood’s rollout controller supports canary, A/B testing, and staged model deployments. Today, mitigation for rollout-induced latency and error regressions is largely manual, relying on on-call detection and intervention. This PRD defines an integrated capability that automatically pauses or rolls back ...
text:
## Overview
Redwood’s rollout controller supports canary, A/B testing, and staged model deployments. Today, mitigation for rollout-induced latency and error regressions is largely manual, relying on on-call detection and intervention. This PRD defines an integrated capability that automatically pauses or rolls back a rollout when SLO monitors detect sustained breaches, scoped to the exact rollout dimensions (route/service, model version, region, customer tier).

This feature is a core reliability differentiator for Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments: it reduces time-to-mitigation and helps prevent SLA violations during aggressive iteration.

## Goals
1) Automatically mitigate rollout regressions by pausing/rolling back when SLOs breach with high confidence.
2) Provide clear auditability: “what happened, why, and what did the controller do?”
3) Provide safe, configurabl...

title: Design Spec: Rollout Controller Integration with SLO Monitoring (Auto-pause/Auto-rollback)
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies how the Rollout Controller will integrate with Redwood’s SLO monitoring system to automatically *pause* or *rollback* a model rollout (canary/A-B/staged) when latency or error-rate SLOs breach within the scope of the rollout.

The design introduces:
- A controller-side **SLO Evalua...
text:
## Summary
This document specifies how the Rollout Controller will integrate with Redwood’s SLO monitoring system to automatically *pause* or *rollback* a model rollout (canary/A-B/staged) when latency or error-rate SLOs breach within the scope of the rollout.

The design introduces:
- A controller-side **SLO Evaluator** that queries rollout-scoped SLO monitors via **slo-toolkit**
- A policy-driven decision engine (pause vs rollback vs suppress)
- A structured **audit trail** for every evaluation/decision
- Console-facing APIs to view auto-actions and reasons

## Goals
1. Automatically mitigate regressions during rollouts using SLO signals:
 - p95/p99 latency (route-scoped)
 - error rate (5xx / upstream failures)
 - optional saturation signals where defined and stable
2. Evaluate breaches using **burn-rate + multi-window** logic with **minimum sample gating** and **debounce** to reduc...

preview:

- Protect revenue integrity and margin while enabling reasonable commercial flexibilit...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s standard rules for **discounts** and **credits** for usage-based products (Hosted API, Dedicated burst/overages where applicable, and any token/embedding-based SKUs). The goals are:
- Protect revenue integrity and margin while enabling reasonable commercial flexibility.
- Ensure discounts/credits are **consistently applied to the same meters** customers see.
- Maintain **audit-ready** approval trails for RevRec (ASC 606) and internal controls.

This policy applies to all Redwood employees and contractors involved in quoting, contracting, billing, customer support remediation, and pricing experiments.

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted API usage SKUs (e.g., billed tokens, embeddings units, rerank units, request-based SKUs if/when used).
- Dedicated: usage-based components (e.g., burst GPU-seconds above commit) and one-time credits applied t...

text:
## Overview
This page defines the empty states and onboarding content for the **Optimization suggestions (beta)** experience in the Redwood Console.

Scope (beta):
- Suggestions list + details drawer
- Empty states for: feature not enabled, access denied, insufficient telemetry, no eligible routes, compute/pipeline issues
- Lightweight onboarding panel (first-time guidance) and setup CTAs

Non-goals:
- Quantization onboarding (may be referenced as “coming soon” but not linked as a primary CTA)
- Any copy that implies guaranteed savings

## UX principles
1) **Be explicit about what’s missing** (data, access, or eligibility), and what the user can do next.
2) **Avoid blame-y language** (“you didn’t configure…”). Prefer neutral, actionable phrasing.
3) **Trust-first**: always include a short disclaimer: “Estimates are directional and based on recent telemetry.”
4) **Keep the page useful*...

## 1. Overview
This spec defines the Redwood Console UX for **Usage Insights**: cost + usage breakdowns by **API route** and **model**, including drilldowns, “Top cost drivers” insights, CSV export, shareable report links, and accessibility requirements.

Primary goals:
- Help developers understand **where spend is coming from** (route + model) and **why** (tokens in/out, cache, retries, fallbacks, context length, latency-correlated spend).
- Support engineering + finance workflows with **exportable and shareable** reports.
- Ensure the Console totals remain consistent with Billing (within tolerance; see PRD + correctness checks).

Non-goals (initial):
- Automated optimization actions (we only surface recommendations/links).
- Scheduled email reports.

## 2. Personas & permissions
### Personas
- **Engineering lead / platform engineer:** wants hotspots by route/model and actionable dri...

text:
Summary
-------
This document summarizes the Redwood Optimize 1.3 release (GA: 2026-02-20). Optimize 1.3 introduces Performance Profiles, Telemetry-based Cost Estimator, latency-tier presets for the Console, and several SDK/CLI usability improvements. The release targets customers using Redwood Optimize to tune Dedicated and Hosted inference for predictable unit economics and simplified rollout of quantization/batching recommendations.

Highlights
----------
- Performance Profiles (feature flag: optimize.performance_profiles): per-model, per-workload profiles that capture measured latency/throughput/cost at multiple sequence-length distributions. Profiles power auto-suggestion rules and the new cost estimator.
- Telemetry-based Cost Estimator: end-to-end estimator that uses historical request shape telemetry (prefix length, tokens-per-request, hit rates) to produce monthly cost projec...

## Purpose
This page aligns **customer-facing SLA language** with Redwood’s **engineered SLIs/SLOs** for:
- **Hosted API (Shared)**
- **Dedicated (Reserved Capacity)**

It is intended for Product, Legal, Sales/Solutions Engineering, Customer Success, and SRE to ensure:
- The SLA is **measurable and auditable** from Redwood telemetry.
- The SLA is **contractually precise** (clear inclusions/exclusions).
- Internal SLOs remain **stricter engineering targets**, while the SLA sets the **customer credit commitment**.

> Note: This doc specifies the *mapping and definitions*. The canonical SLI formulas and metric sources are owned by SRE in the linked SLI/SLO standards pages.

---

title: PRD: Standard Cost Model Spec + Internal Cost Model API (v1)
preview:
h1. Summary

Redwood Inference currently computes and displays “cost” in multiple places (Console dashboards, billing hooks, Optimize prototypes, finance reporting). Each path uses slightly different definitions and inputs (prefill vs decode accounting, KV cache attribution, batching efficiency, quantization effects...
text:
h1. Summary

Redwood Inference currently computes and displays “cost” in multiple places (Console dashboards, billing hooks, Optimize prototypes, finance reporting). Each path uses slightly different definitions and inputs (prefill vs decode accounting, KV cache attribution, batching efficiency, quantization effects, overhead allocation, and handling of telemetry lag). This results in mismatched dashboards vs invoices, inconsistent ROI estimates, and friction during enterprise/security/finance reviews.

This PRD defines a single, versioned Standard Cost Model Spec and an internal Cost Model API (service + shared client library) that produces canonical cost primitives used by:
- Redwood Console: consistent, explorable cost breakdown views
- Redwood Optimize: defensible “what-if” projections for batching/caching/quantization recommendations
- Finance/FP&A: stable unit-economics reportin...

This PRD defines objectives, target audiences (procurement-led vs PLG), key AWS/GCP marketplace constraints, succe...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference will refresh its AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace presence to better support enterprise procurement paths and reduce onboarding friction for marketplace-driven customers. This PRD defines objectives, target audiences (procurement-led vs PLG), key AWS/GCP marketplace constraints, success metrics, and a phased rollout plan.

This work includes:
- Replacing legacy listing content with current positioning (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private + add-ons).
- Introducing marketplace SKUs that map cleanly to internal billing meters.
- Publishing a marketplace-ready security documentation bundle suitable for AWS/GCP security review and accelerating customer security questionnaires.
- Improving the end-to-end onboarding flow: subscription → entitlement → Redwood org provisioning → first API key → first successful request → metering/invoicing.

## Background / Why now
M...

title: PRD: Optimization Suggestions (Console UX) — Beta (Batching + Caching)
preview:
## Overview
Redwood Inference will ship a beta “Optimization suggestions” experience in the Redwood Console to help customers reduce inference cost while managing latency and reliability risk. The beta focuses on a new **Suggestions** view that surfaces **batching** and **caching** recommendations.

The Suggestions ...
text:
## Overview
Redwood Inference will ship a beta “Optimization suggestions” experience in the Redwood Console to help customers reduce inference cost while managing latency and reliability risk. The beta focuses on a new **Suggestions** view that surfaces **batching** and **caching** recommendations.

The Suggestions view must:
- Make cost optimization actionable: highlight top opportunities per route/model, with a clear “why” and “how to enable”.
- Build trust: present conservative estimates, show assumptions/inputs, and communicate risk consistently.
- Create a closed loop: instrument impressions → expand → dismiss → apply interactions to learn what is useful.

This PRD describes the beta UX scope, requirements, success metrics, GA criteria, and staged rollout plan.

---

## Problem statement
Customers know there are meaningful cost savings available via batching/caching tuning, but t...

title: Cost Model API v1 — Design Spec
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies Cost Model API v1 for Redwood Inference. The API provides a single, canonical interface to compute and serve standardized inference cost primitives for:
- Redwood Console (dashboards, cost breakdown views)
- Redwood Optimize (what-if projections and recommendation ROI estimates)
- ...
text:
## Summary
This document specifies Cost Model API v1 for Redwood Inference. The API provides a single, canonical interface to compute and serve standardized inference cost primitives for:
- Redwood Console (dashboards, cost breakdown views)
- Redwood Optimize (what-if projections and recommendation ROI estimates)
- Finance/unit-economics reporting (through shared primitives; not the billing system of record)

The v1 API is internal-only (service-to-service) and is designed to be:
- Deterministic (given identical inputs, schema version, and policy)
- Bounded (responses and computation time must remain bounded under high-cardinality workloads)
- Versioned (schema + semantics are versioned; compatible evolution is enforced)

## Goals (v1)
1) Provide stable endpoints for:
 - Per-request cost estimate
 - Aggregated cost breakdowns over a time window
 - What-if projections for config change...

title: PRD: In-console Usage Insights (Route + Model Cost Breakdown)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly need to understand and control inference spend at a granular level (by API route and model). Today, cost visibility is primarily invoice-level and/or coarse usage charts that don’t explain *why* spend changed. This PRD proposes “In-console Usage Insights”: a set of...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly need to understand and control inference spend at a granular level (by API route and model). Today, cost visibility is primarily invoice-level and/or coarse usage charts that don’t explain *why* spend changed. This PRD proposes “In-console Usage Insights”: a set of Redwood Console views and exportable reports that attribute usage and cost by **route + model**, highlight **top cost drivers**, and provide **“what changed?”** deltas, with totals reconciled to billing.

This is a production-focused cost transparency feature: reliable totals, clear definitions, RBAC controls, and performance that supports interactive debugging by engineering teams.

---

## Problem statement
### Current pain
Customers and internal teams struggle to answer:
- “Which endpoint is costing us the most?”
- “Did our spend spike because token output increased, ca...

title: PRD: Shadow Traffic for Quality Eval Metrics (Console & Rollouts)
preview:
## Summary
Teams trust Redwood for safe rollouts based on latency/cost/error SLOs, but still lack a production-safe way to detect *quality* regressions (correctness, safety, instruction following, format adherence) when changing models/versions/quantization/prompt templates. This PRD proposes **Shadow Traffic**: dup...
text:
## Summary
Teams trust Redwood for safe rollouts based on latency/cost/error SLOs, but still lack a production-safe way to detect *quality* regressions (correctness, safety, instruction following, format adherence) when changing models/versions/quantization/prompt templates. This PRD proposes **Shadow Traffic**: duplicate a sampled subset of live requests to one or more candidate variants, score outputs asynchronously, and surface **quality deltas** alongside existing performance and error metrics—without affecting the user-visible response.

Shadow Traffic is designed to work across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments using existing routing/rollout primitives (route configs, canaries, A/B) and Redwood’s eval-harness scoring templates.

---

## Problem statement
### What customers do today
- Run offline prompt sets that do not match production distribution.
- Manual...

title: Console UX Spec — Shadow Experiments (Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics)
preview:
## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting th...
text:
## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting the response returned to the end user.

This document specifies the **Console UX** for:
- Creating and managing Shadow Experiments
- Viewing results (quality deltas, confidence intervals, segments)
- Drilling into examples with safe data handling
- Attaching quality signals to Rollouts/Canaries

### 1.1 Goals
- Make it safe and obvious that shadowing does **not** change user responses and is **fail-open**.
- Help engineers answer: “Is the candidate variant materially worse/better on quality, and are we statistically confident?”
- Provide clear integration with Rollouts so qua...

The demo tenant is a production-like, isolated environment with pinned model versions, seeded synthetic usage, and automation that enables any AE/SE to run...
text:
h1. Overview

Redwood Inference is standing up a reliable, repeatable sales demo environment ("demo tenant") paired with a scripted talk track and standard deck. The demo tenant is a production-like, isolated environment with pinned model versions, seeded synthetic usage, and automation that enables any AE/SE to run a consistent end-to-end demo with minimal setup and high resilience to common failure modes.

This PRD defines:
* Primary users and user stories (AE/SE + internal support roles)
* Required end-to-end demo scenarios
* Non-functional requirements (reliability, reset time, data safety, security)
* “Backup mode” requirements (screenshots, recordings, canned outputs)
* Success metrics and acceptance criteria

h2. Goals

* Make demos consistent across AEs/SEs, regions, and customer segments (Hosted API + Dedicated controls + Private/VPC narrative).
* Reduce time-to-ready for a d...

title: Optimize Quality Gates — System Design (v1)
preview:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end system design for **Quality Guardrails Gating for Optimize**. The feature ensures that any Optimize recommendation that changes serving behavior (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profiles) is **blocked from production traffic** until it passes ...
text:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end system design for **Quality Guardrails Gating for Optimize**. The feature ensures that any Optimize recommendation that changes serving behavior (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profiles) is **blocked from production traffic** until it passes required quality evaluations (offline gates + optional online canary signals). The design includes:
- Service boundaries and interactions (Optimize, eval-harness, Console, Rollout Controller)
- Data model for gate runs and baselines
- APIs and contracts
- Gate run state machine: `pending` → `running` → `passed|failed` with `canceled|timed_out` and `overridden`
- Failure modes, retries, and operational guardrails

## Goals
1. **Prevent silent quality regressions** from cost optimizations.
2. Provide **consistent pass/fail gates** with statistical handling for variance and fl...
